claimed_contribution: Introduces a new incentive-aligned upgrading method for eliciting preferences in complex products that combines advantages of self-explicated and conjoint-style approaches.
Implements a Web-based, one-attribute-at-a-time upgrading procedure with BDM-based willingness-to-pay elicitation so truthful reporting is in participants' interest.
Shows empirically, in a digital camera application, that the upgrading method yields better predictive performance than the self-explicated benchmark and reveals different preference structure.
context: The empirical application is a Web-based consumer preference study for digital cameras, a complex product category with many attributes and levels. Participants were university students at a major U.S. university.
design_and_data: The study uses a within-subject experiment with 88 participants, comparing a self-explicated task and an upgrading task, with task order randomized across four conditions. The product space includes 11 digital-camera attributes and 60 levels; participants also completed two external holdout choice tasks with 17 camera options each (plus a no-purchase option) and a short survey. In upgrading, participants started from a bare-bones camera, selected attributes to upgrade one at a time, and stated WTP for desired levels; random cutoff prices (BDM-style) determined upgrade outcomes, with two mandatory sets and optional extra sets. Upgrading data were estimated with a hierarchical Bayesian logit using paired comparisons, and predictions were evaluated out of sample.
main_findings: The upgrading method achieved higher out-of-sample prediction accuracy than the self-explicated method in both holdout tasks (42% vs 27% for computer-generated profiles, and 26% vs 19% for Bestbuy.com profiles; naive baseline 6%).
Modeling upgrading responses with an inequality interpretation produced better predictive performance than an indifference-equality interpretation.
Compared with self-explicated responses, upgrading-based estimates indicated quantitatively different partworth patterns, including more nonlinear within-attribute preferences for some attributes.
one_line_summary: This paper proposes a Web-based, incentive-aligned upgrading method for complex products and shows in a digital camera experiment that it predicts choices better than the standard self-explicated approach.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior preference-measurement work by addressing the complex-product setting where full conjoint tasks are burdensome and by improving on self-explicated methods that are easy to use but not incentive aligned.
research_gap: The paper argues that existing methods leave a gap for complex products: conjoint analysis becomes impractical with many attributes and levels, while the widely used self-explicated method has compositional limitations and lacks incentive alignment. It seeks a method that is realistic, cognitively manageable, and truth-inducing.
research_question: Can a Web-based, one-attribute-at-a-time upgrading mechanism with BDM incentives elicit truthful attribute preferences and willingness to pay for complex products? Does this method provide better external predictive validity than the benchmark self-explicated approach?
